citation: Understanding FDA Regulatory Requirements for Investigational New Drug Applications for Sponsor-Investigators. PMC4435682.
takeaway: Peer-reviewed walkthrough of the sponsor-investigator dual-role IND regulatory burden - good grounding reference for the OSSICRO wiki IND section

citation: Bhagat S, Kapatkar V, Katare S, et al. Potential Risks and Mitigation Strategies During the Conduct of a Clinical Trial: An Industry Perspective. Rev Recent Clin Trials. 2018;13(1):52-60.
takeaway: Industry-sourced risk/mitigation taxonomy across the conduct phase (site master file/TMF prep, IP shipment, recruitment, SAE monitoring, monitoring visits, CRF/data query flow, close-out) — directly maps to a sponsor-investigator's essential-document and monitoring-plan structure.

citation: Grady CL, Meyers L, Le C, et al. A web-based clinical trial management system for a sham-controlled multicenter clinical trial in depression. Clin Trials. 2010;7(2):174-82.
takeaway: Case study of building a custom CTMS for a multicenter academic trial — architecture, timeline and lessons useful for a sponsor-investigator weighing off-the-shelf vs. custom document/data systems.

citation: A checklist for clinical trials in rare disease: obstacles and anticipatory actions — lessons learned from the FOR-DMD trial. Trials. 2018;19(1):291.
takeaway: Practical obstacle/anticipatory-action checklist from a multinational academic rare-disease trial, spanning IND, sIRB, site activation, and drug supply — a ready-made risk checklist template.

citation: Improving readiness for recruitment through simulated trial activation: the Adjuvant Steroids in Adults with Pandemic influenza (ASAP) trial. Trials. 2017;18(1):546.
takeaway: Introduces 'simulated trial activation' as a dry-run mechanism to compress site-activation timelines for emergency/pandemic-response trials — a reusable process for rapid-startup IIT protocols.

citation: Kim ES, Lee IK, Chun J, et al. Decentralized clinical trials for medications to reduce the risk of dementia: Consensus report and guidance. Alzheimers Dement. 2024;20(7):4625-4634.
takeaway: Multi-stakeholder consensus guidance on which DCT elements (remote consent, home visits, digital endpoints) are and are not feasible in a specific disease context — a template for domain-specific DCT feasibility assessment.

citation: Decentralized clinical trials (DCTs): A few ethical considerations. Front Public Health. 2022;10:1081150.
takeaway: Surveys consent, oversight, and equity concerns specific to decentralization (remote consent validity, local-site IRB reliance, participant burden) — relevant to OSSICRO's consent-module conditional logic for DCT designs.

citation: The value of decentralized clinical trials: Inclusion, accessibility, and innovation. Science. 2024;385(6711):eadq4994.
takeaway: High-level policy perspective arguing DCT elements materially improve enrollment diversity and access — useful framing for why a sponsor-investigator platform should support DCT-hybrid designs, not just brick-and-mortar site models.

citation: Stakeholder perspectives on adaptive clinical trials: a scoping review. Trials. 2020;21(1):539.
takeaway: Scoping review of barriers to adaptive-design uptake (statistical literacy, DSMB unblinding logistics, regulatory unfamiliarity) — informs adaptive-design guardrails a sponsor-investigator protocol template should flag.

citation: Rathi VK, Krumholz HM. A systematic review of methods used to conduct decentralised clinical trials. Br J Clin Pharmacol. 2022;88(6):2843-2862.
takeaway: Systematic catalogue of DCT operational methods (eConsent platforms, direct-to-participant drug shipment, telehealth visits, wearables) — a checklist for which vendor/process categories OSSICRO's document set needs to account for.

citation: Decentralized Clinical Trials as a New Paradigm of Trial Delivery to Improve Equity of Access. JAMA Oncol. 2024;10(4):526-530.
takeaway: Oncology-focused viewpoint on equity gains and implementation barriers of DCT elements, with FDA guidance context (2023 draft DCT guidance) — good source for regulatory citation trail on DCT guidance evolution.

citation: Assessment of digital therapeutics in decentralized clinical trials: A scoping review. PLOS Digit Health. 2025;4(6):e0000905.
takeaway: Maps how digital therapeutics trials specifically use DCT infrastructure — narrower but highly current (2025) evidence base for software-as-intervention IITs.

citation: Grimes DA, Bassler D, Alberto Rada Fernández de Jáuregui G, et al. Risk-Based Monitoring in Clinical Trials: Past, Present, and Future. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2021;55(4):899-906.
takeaway: Traces RBM from the 2013 FDA guidance through ICH E6(R2) to present practice — the canonical historical/regulatory-basis reference for an RBM module.

citation: Risk-based Monitoring of Clinical Trials: An Integrative Approach. Clin Ther. 2018;40(7):1204-1212.
takeaway: Proposes an integrative RBM framework combining centralized statistical monitoring with targeted on-site visits — a template monitoring-plan structure by risk tier.

citation: Risk-based centralized data monitoring of clinical trials at the time of COVID-19 pandemic. Contemp Clin Trials. 2021;104:106368.
takeaway: Documents pandemic-era acceleration of centralized/remote monitoring adoption — relevant precedent for remote-monitoring SOPs a sponsor-investigator may need when on-site visits aren't feasible.

citation: Risk-Based Data Monitoring: Quality Control in Central Nervous System (CNS) Clinical Trials. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2018;53(2):176-182.
takeaway: Disease-area-specific (CNS) RBM quality-control example illustrating how key-risk-indicator selection should be tailored to endpoint type — a worked example for OSSICRO's monitoring-plan generator.

citation: Statistical challenges for central monitoring in clinical trials: a review. Int J Clin Oncol. 2016;21(1):28-37.
takeaway: Statistical methods (outlier detection, digit-preference tests) underlying central statistical monitoring — technical basis for any automated central-monitoring feature.

citation: Effectiveness of remote risk-based monitoring and potential benefits for combination with direct data capture. Trials. 2024;25(1):384.
takeaway: Recent (2024) evidence that combining remote RBM with direct data capture (eSource) further reduces monitoring burden — supports an eSource-first monitoring-plan default.

citation: Perceived barriers and facilitators to Risk Based Monitoring in academic-led clinical trials: a mixed methods study. Trials. 2017;18(1):423.
takeaway: Directly studies academic (non-industry) sites' experience adopting RBM — the closest analog in the literature to a sponsor-investigator/small-site RBM adoption problem, including resourcing barriers.

citation: Risk-Based Monitoring Approach to Ensure the Quality of Clinical Study Data and Enable Effective Monitoring. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2020;54(1):139-143.
takeaway: Concise practitioner-oriented RBM implementation approach; good as a plain-language basis for an RBM plan-generation wizard.

citation: Transitioning to the National Institutes of Health single institutional review board model: Piloting the use of the Streamlined, Multi-site, Accelerated Resources for Trials IRB Reliance. Clin Trials. 2019;16(3):290-296.
takeaway: First-hand pilot report on standing up SMART IRB reliance infrastructure ahead of the 2018 NIH sIRB mandate — operational precedent for reliance-agreement workflow design.

citation: Meeting the Challenge: The National Cancer Institute's Central Institutional Review Board for Multi-Site Research. J Clin Oncol. 2018;36(8):819-824.
takeaway: NCI CIRB as a long-running large-scale sIRB model predating the NIH policy — informative for reliance-agreement variants beyond SMART IRB (cooperative-group model).

citation: The reality of informed consent: empirical studies on patient comprehension-systematic review. Trials. 2021;22(1):57.
takeaway: Systematic review confirming persistently low comprehension rates on core consent elements (randomization, placebo, voluntariness) — strongest evidence basis for building comprehension-check logic into an eConsent module.

citation: Quality of Informed Consent in Phase III Clinical Trials in Portugal: The Participants' Perspective. Acta Med Port. 2024;37(9):601-608.
takeaway: Recent (2024) participant-perspective study reaffirming comprehension gaps in a well-regulated EU setting — shows the problem persists under mature regulatory regimes, not just historically.

citation: Quality of informed consent in cancer clinical trials in India: A cross-sectional survey. Natl Med J India. 2018;31(6):334-338.
takeaway: Cross-national comparator on consent-quality survey methodology, useful if OSSICRO needs a validated consent-quality assessment instrument.

citation: Informed Consent in IBD Trials: Where We Are and Where We Need to Go. Inflamm Bowel Dis. 2019;25(7):1115-1119.
takeaway: Disease-specific consent-improvement recommendations (teach-back, layered consent) directly reusable as consent-template best practices.

citation: Joffe S, Cook EF, Cleary PD, Clark JW, Weeks JC. Quality of informed consent in cancer clinical trials: a cross-sectional survey. Lancet. 2001;358(9295):1772-7.
takeaway: The foundational, most-cited empirical study establishing that consent-form length correlates poorly (or inversely) with comprehension — the historical anchor citation for any consent-quality discussion.

citation: Improvement of informed consent and the quality of consent documents. Lancet Oncol. 2008;9(5):485-93.
takeaway: Review of interventions (readability editing, multimedia, teach-back) shown to actually improve consent-document quality — a direct source of design requirements for a consent-authoring template.

citation: Voluntary informed consent in research and clinical care: an update. Pain Pract. 2008;8(6):446-51.
takeaway: Reviews voluntariness/coercion concerns distinct from comprehension — relevant to consent-process (not just document) checklist items, e.g., time-to-decide, undue-influence screening.

citation: Vasan A, Bergs R, Brandel P, et al. Harvard Catalyst | The Clinical Translational Science Center IND/IDE Consult Service: providing an IND/IDE consult service in a decentralized network of academic healthcare centers. Clin Transl Sci. 2014;7(2):150-5.
takeaway: Operational description of an institutional IND/IDE regulatory-consult service model — a template for the kind of structured regulatory-support workflow OSSICRO is trying to substitute/automate for solo sponsor-investigators.

citation: Barnes M, Bierer BE, Kaiser Permanente, et al. Support for investigator-initiated clinical research involving investigational drugs or devices: the Clinical and Translational Science Award experience. Acad Med. 2011;86(2):217-23.
takeaway: CTSA-network survey of what institutional infrastructure (regulatory writing support, IND-navigation help, monitoring plans) investigator-initiated-trial PIs actually need — a needs-assessment source directly on-point for OSSICRO's feature scoping.

citation: Miller JP, Moxley RT 3rd, Aggarwal R, et al. Challenges in drug development for muscle disease: a stakeholders' meeting. Muscle Nerve. 2007;35(1):8-16.
takeaway: Multi-stakeholder (academic, FDA, industry) meeting report on rare-disease IND/regulatory obstacles — illustrates the negotiation dynamic a sponsor-investigator has with FDA on novel-endpoint programs.

citation: Challenges for academic investigator-initiated pediatric trials for rare diseases. Clin Ther. 2014;36(2):184-90.
takeaway: Names concrete academic-IIT pain points (funding gaps, regulatory-writing expertise deficits, small-N statistics) in a pediatric rare-disease context — a needs list to validate OSSICRO's feature set against.

citation: Investigator-initiated studies: Challenges and solutions. Perspect Clin Res. 2018;9(4):179-183.
takeaway: General-purpose IIT challenges/solutions review (funding, regulatory compliance burden, monitoring capacity, data management) — good overview citation for OSSICRO's problem-statement section.

citation: Management of pharmacovigilance during the COVID-19 pandemic crisis by the safety department of an academic sponsor: Lessons learnt and challenges from the EU DisCoVeRy clinical trial. Pharmacol Res Perspect. 2023;11(3):e01072.
takeaway: Rare first-hand account of an academic (non-industry) sponsor running its own pharmacovigilance department for a large multinational trial — directly relevant operational template for sponsor-investigator PV responsibilities.

citation: Pharmacovigilance in Clinical Trials: Current Practice and Challenges. Account Res. 2016;23(1):23-30.
takeaway: Surveys inconsistent SAE-reporting practice across sponsor types — supports building a standardized, protocol-agnostic safety-reporting decision tree in OSSICRO.

citation: [Recording and reporting adverse reactions in clinical trials. New legal provisions according to the 12th Law Amending the German Drug Law (AMG) and the Ordinance on GCP (GCP-V)]. Bundesgesundheitsblatt. 2005;48(2):173-80.
takeaway: Comparative regulatory-law perspective (EU/German AMG/GCP-V) on expedited safety reporting obligations — useful if OSSICRO ever needs a non-US regulatory-reporting variant, and as a model for how reporting-timeline rules get codified into law.

citation: Evolving Standards: Good Clinical Practice Insights from US FDA, MHRA UK, and Health Canada. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2025;119(5):1172-1178.
takeaway: Very recent (2025) tri-regulator comparison of GCP inspection focus areas — current-state benchmark for what FDA/MHRA/Health Canada inspectors are actually emphasizing now, useful to keep OSSICRO's inspection-readiness checklist current.

citation: Shapley S, O'Shaughnessy J, Woodcock J. Center for Drug Evaluation and Research Perspective on Quality in Clinical Trials. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2017;51(4):416-418.
takeaway: FDA CDER's own stated rationale for the quality-by-design / ICH E6(R2) addendum shift — primary-source framing for why OSSICRO should build quality-by-design (critical-to-quality factors) into protocol templates rather than bolt on compliance after the fact.

citation: Data and Safety Monitoring Board Best Practices in Clinical Trials. Cureus. 2026;18(4):e107661.
takeaway: Current, freely accessible DSMB operations primer (charter content, meeting cadence, interim-analysis unblinding procedures, communication pathways to sponsor) — directly usable as the basis for a DSMB-charter template.

citation: Key concepts of clinical trials: a narrative review. Postgrad Med. 2011;123(5):194-204.
takeaway: General narrative review of trial-design fundamentals (randomization, blinding, DSMB role, stopping rules) — a useful plain-language reference for an OSSICRO onboarding/glossary section aimed at physician-investigators new to the sponsor role.

citation: Science 37 Holdings, Inc., Form 8-K (SEC EDGAR filing 0001819113-24-007463), Jan 29 2024
takeaway: Definitive primary-source documentation that eMed's all-cash tender for Science 37 valued the company at ~$38M (vs. a $1.05-1.33B 2021 SPAC valuation) — the canonical failed pure-DCT-disintermediation data point for OSSICRO's business-case background.

citation: LifeSci Acquisition II Corp., Form 8-K (SEC EDGAR filing 0001819113-21-062780), May 7 2021
takeaway: Original SPAC merger agreement establishing Science 37's peak $1.05B enterprise valuation — needed as the 'before' half of the collapse comparison.

citation: Clinical Research News Online, 'As CVS Slowly Exits Clinical Trial Business, Walgreens Continues Ramp-Up', May 23 2023
takeaway: CVS's retail-pharmacy clinical trials unit (launched 2021, grew 10x year-over-year, ran ~200 in-home trials) still wound down entirely by end-2024, while Walgreens' comparable unit persisted — evidence that retail-disintermediation failure is company-specific execution, not proof the decentralized-site model itself is unviable (FDA's DCT draft guidance explicitly names pharmacies as valid non-traditional sites).

citation: Nahin RL, Ely S, et al. "NIAID ClinRegs—a Public Database of Country Clinical Research Regulatory and Ethics Requirements: Design and Utilization Analysis." Clin Infect Dis. 2021;73(7):1296.
takeaway: Describes ClinRegs' 36-topic-area/23-country structure and design rationale — useful as a schema reference if OSSICRO models international-site regulatory-requirement lookups.

citation: "The NIH StrokeNet: A User's Guide." PMC.
takeaway: Documents the StrokeNet governance/protocol-approval chain (Protocol PI -> NCC/NDMC -> cIRB -> StrokeNet DSMB -> FDA IND/IDE amendment) — a concrete sequencing model for a coordinated multi-site network trial that OSSICRO's workflow engine could mirror for the Micro-CRO/sponsor-investigator handoffs.

citation: Cobb N, et al. The IRB Reliance Exchange (IREx): A national web-based platform for operationalizing single IRB review. Journal of Clinical and Translational Science / PMC9066316, 2022.
takeaway: Describes the architecture and operational workflow of IREx, VUMC's free national platform for documenting and coordinating sIRB reliance — directly relevant to modeling OSSICRO's own reliance-tracking data model (reviewing IRB / relying site / study-team roles, document flow for continuing review and amendments).

citation: Yuan C, Ryan PB, Ta C, et al. Criteria2Query: a natural language interface to clinical databases for cohort definition. J Am Med Inform Assoc. 2019;26(4):294-305.
takeaway: Foundational Criteria2Query paper — establishes the NLP pipeline that parses free-text ClinicalTrials.gov eligibility criteria into OMOP-CDM-encoded structured cohort definitions; directly usable as a design reference for OSSICRO's matching engine.

citation: Chan AW, Tetzlaff JM, Altman DG, et al. SPIRIT 2013 Statement: Defining Standard Protocol Items for Clinical Trials. Ann Intern Med. 2013;158(3):200-207.
takeaway: 34-item minimum-content checklist for a trial protocol; the backbone the NIH-FDA template and ICH M11 both operationalize. Validate any OSSICRO protocol-generator against this checklist before validating against ICH M11 fields.

citation: Chan AW, Tetzlaff JM, Gøtzsche PC, et al. SPIRIT 2013 Explanation and Elaboration: Guidance for protocols of clinical trials. BMJ. 2013;346:e7586.
takeaway: Item-by-item rationale plus a model example drawn from a real protocol for each of the 33 checklist items — directly reusable as inline help text in an OSSICRO protocol-authoring UI.

citation: SPIRIT 2025 statement: Updated guideline for protocols of randomised trials. Simultaneously published Lancet/Nature Medicine/JAMA/BMJ, 2025.
takeaway: Supersedes 2013: adds 2 items, revises 5, deletes/merges 5, adds a dedicated open-science section and a patient/public-involvement item — the current target for any new protocol-template validator.

citation: CONSORT 2025 statement: Updated guideline for reporting randomised trials. Simultaneously published, 2025.
takeaway: 30-item reporting checklist (up from 25 in 2010), 7 new items incl. an open-science section; pairs with SPIRIT 2025 for a full protocol-to-report lifecycle template set.

citation: Page MJ, McKenzie JE, Bossuyt PM, et al. The PRISMA 2020 statement: an updated guideline for reporting systematic reviews. BMJ. 2021;372:n71.
takeaway: Relevant to OSSICRO's literature-review/background-section generation and to any meta-analysis module across accumulated n-of-1 or small-cohort trials.

citation: Hoffmann TC, Glasziou PP, Boutron I, et al. Better reporting of interventions: template for intervention description and replication (TIDieR) checklist and guide. BMJ. 2014;348:g1687.
takeaway: 12-item checklist for describing an intervention with enough granularity to replicate it — maps directly to the 'Intervention' section a sponsor-investigator protocol must specify, especially for behavioral/device/procedural interventions where FDA templates are thinner than for drugs.

citation: Council for International Organizations of Medical Sciences (CIOMS). Working Groups III and V. Guidelines for Preparing Core Clinical-Safety Information on Drugs, 2nd ed. Geneva: CIOMS.
takeaway: Defines Company Core Safety Information (CCSI) / Company Core Data Sheet methodology that underlies labeling-based 'expectedness' determinations used throughout E2A/E2D SUSAR logic — essential for OSSICRO's expectedness-check engine.

citation: CIOMS Working Group VI. Management of Safety Information from Clinical Trials. Geneva: CIOMS, 2005.
takeaway: Pre-ICH-E2F reference for aggregate clinical trial safety reporting practice; still cited by DSUR authors as a good-practice supplement, useful for OSSICRO's DSUR template annotations.

citation: WHO-Uppsala Monitoring Centre. The Use of the WHO-UMC System for Standardised Case Causality Assessment.
takeaway: Canonical 6-category causality scale (certain/probable-likely/possible/unlikely/conditional-unclassified/unassessable-unclassifiable) that OSSICRO's causality-assessment module should implement directly rather than reinvent.

citation: Rare Diseases Clinical Research Network's Organization and Approach to Observational Research and Health Outcomes Research. PMC4124127.
takeaway: Describes the RDCRN federated-database/eCRF architecture that a Micro-CRO data layer for rare-disease natural-history studies could mirror.

citation: Evaluating Personalized (N-of-1) Trials in Rare Diseases: How Much Experimentation Is Enough? Harvard Data Science Review, Special Issue 3 (Personalized N-of-1 Trials).
takeaway: Identifies an open methodological gap (no consensus on adequate experimentation length) directly relevant to OSSICRO's n-of-1/single-patient protocol templates for rare disease.

citation: A methodological review of randomised n-of-1 trials. PMC11020886.
takeaway: Systematic review of n-of-1 design/analysis practice; useful as the evidence base behind an n-of-1 protocol template.

citation: A Two-Stage Patient-Focused Study Design for Rare Disease Controlled Trials. arXiv:1607.00046.
takeaway: Alternative small-N controlled-trial design usable when a full RCT is infeasible for an ultra-rare indication — candidate for the protocol-template library.

citation: Feehan AK, Garcia-Diaz J. Investigator Responsibilities in Clinical Research. Ochsner J. 2020. DOI: 10.31486/toj.19.0085 (PMID 32284682, PMC7122254)
takeaway: Systematic walkthrough of what signing FDA Form 1572 legally obligates a PI to (21 CFR duties, delegation limits, FDA-inspection consequences up to criminal prosecution) — direct primary-source input for an OSSICRO 1572-obligations module.

citation: So You Want to Be a Principal Investigator. J Oncol Pract. 2018. DOI: 10.1200/JOP.18.00011 (PMID 29750578)
takeaway: Practical primer on the operational and regulatory burden a first-time PI/sponsor-investigator takes on — useful for OSSICRO onboarding-checklist content.

citation: Ebrahimi H, et al. Practical Guide to Clinical Trial Accessibility: Making Trial Participation a Standard of Care. Am Soc Clin Oncol Educ Book. 2025. DOI: 10.1200/EDBK-25-100052 (PMID 40388680)
takeaway: Multi-stakeholder consensus piece naming overly restrictive eligibility criteria, protocol complexity, and investigator burden as the core structural barriers to trial accessibility — directly supports OSSICRO's eligibility-criteria review workflow design rationale.

citation: Mitchell AK, et al. Analysis of eligibility criteria in Alzheimer's and related dementias clinical trials. Sci Rep. 2024;14:15036. DOI: 10.1038/s41598-024-65767-x (PMID 38951633, PMC11217383)
takeaway: 196-trial NIA-funded audit: median 15 eligibility criteria per trial, 82% contain poorly operationalized (ambiguous) criteria, 42% English-speakers-only — concrete quantification of the eligibility-criteria quality problem OSSICRO's template engine should guard against.

citation: Xu J, et al. Machine learning enabled subgroup analysis with real-world data to inform clinical trial eligibility criteria design. Sci Rep. 2023;13:613. DOI: 10.1038/s41598-023-27856-1 (PMID 36635438, PMC9837131)
takeaway: Data-driven approach to deriving exclusion criteria from real EHR-based adverse-event subphenotypes rather than boilerplate — a model for an OSSICRO 'evidence-informed exclusion criteria' generator.

citation: Sun Y, et al. A Framework for Systematic Assessment of Clinical Trial Population Representativeness Using Electronic Health Records Data. Appl Clin Inform. 2021;12:816-825. DOI: 10.1055/s-0041-1733846 (PMID 34496418, PMC8426045)
takeaway: 85.7% of COVID-19 trials and 30.1% of T2DM trials studied had poor population representativeness under their own stated eligibility criteria — quantifies the over-restriction problem across a large trial sample.

citation: Titz M, et al. Generalisability of randomised trials evaluating perioperative beta-blocker therapy in noncardiac surgery. Br J Anaesth. 2020;125:926-934. DOI: 10.1016/j.bja.2020.08.006 (PMID 32888632, PMC9756304)
takeaway: Only 6.5% average of a real surgical population matched the combined eligibility criteria of 36 published RCTs on the same question — one of the starkest empirical demonstrations of eligibility over-restriction driving recruitment failure.

citation: He Z, Chen Z, Bian J. Analysis of Temporal Constraints in Qualitative Eligibility Criteria of Cancer Clinical Studies. Proc IEEE Int Conf Bioinformatics Biomed. 2016:717-722. DOI: 10.1109/BIBM.2016.7822607 (PMID 29263940, PMC5733789)
takeaway: NLP analysis of >10,800 cancer trials found restrictive temporal qualifiers (e.g., 'within 4 weeks of...') are pervasively over-applied in qualitative eligibility text, independent of clinical necessity.

citation: Kong AM, et al. Considerations for emulations of randomized controlled trials using real-world data: learnings from an emulation of MONALEESA-2. J Comp Eff Res. 2025;14(11):e250026. DOI: 10.57264/cer-2025-0026 (PMID 41147847, PMC12580918)
takeaway: Failed RWD emulation of a positive breast-cancer RCT — trial population systematically younger/healthier/less metastatic-burden than real-world treated patients, illustrating downstream external-validity risk from restrictive entry criteria.

citation: Linke M, et al. Ten years of Central IRB review for an NIH-funded large clinical trial network by an academic IRB: The NIH StrokeNet experience. J Clin Transl Sci. 2026. DOI: 10.1017/cts.2025.10231 (PMID 41657754, PMC12873484)
takeaway: Decade-long operational case study of a single-IRB model run by an academic center for a national multi-site network — direct process template for an OSSICRO sIRB-reliance module.

citation: Stakeholder Views on Novel Consent Forms for an Acute Stroke Trial. Ethics Hum Res. 2025. DOI: 10.1002/eahr.60015 (PMID 40084682)
takeaway: Qualitative stakeholder study on redesigning consent-form format/content for time-pressured acute trials — informs OSSICRO's consent-template variants for emergent/urgent conditions.

citation: Christian MC, et al. Meeting the Challenge: The National Cancer Institute's Central Institutional Review Board for Multi-Site Research. J Clin Oncol. 2018;36(11):1073-1080. DOI: 10.1200/JCO.2017.76.9836 (PMID 29384720)
takeaway: History and operating model of NCI CIRB, the longest-running U.S. single-IRB system for cooperative-group cancer trials — foundational reference for sIRB design and its documented efficiency gains.

citation: Costs and benefits of the National Cancer Institute Central Institutional Review Board. J Clin Oncol. 2009. DOI: 10.1200/JCO.2009.23.2470 (PMID 19841324)
takeaway: Earliest quantified cost-benefit analysis of the sIRB model in oncology — baseline economic evidence for the sIRB-vs-local-IRB efficiency argument OSSICRO's IRB-routing feature should cite.

citation: Comparative study to evaluate quality of data documentation between investigator-initiated and pharmaceutical industry-sponsored studies. Perspect Clin Res. 2019. DOI: 10.4103/picr.PICR_122_18 (PMID 32154144)
takeaway: Head-to-head empirical comparison finding measurable documentation-quality gaps for investigator-initiated trials relative to industry-sponsored ones — the single most directly on-point IIT-quality paper found and a strong justification for OSSICRO's document-completeness engine.

citation: Bertagnolli MC, et al. VIR-SAAF: national decentralised RCT of home-based sleep apnoea screening in atrial fibrillation (Denmark). BMJ Open. 2026. DOI: 10.1136/bmjopen-2026-121645 (PMID 42409410)
takeaway: Current (2026) fully decentralized national-scale RCT protocol — good current-state reference for a fully home-based DCT design pattern.

citation: Lessons learned and recommendations from the Canadian adaptive platform trial of treatments for COVID in community settings (CanTreatCOVID). Contemp Clin Trials. 2026. DOI: 10.1016/j.cct.2026.108303 (PMID 41985658)
takeaway: Post-hoc operational lessons-learned from a national decentralized/community adaptive-platform trial — direct source of DCT conduct pitfalls and fixes.

citation: Decentralized community-integrated research sites drive higher randomization rates: insights from a large-scale neurodegenerative disease trial. Front Med (Lausanne). 2025;12:1623776. DOI: 10.3389/fmed.2025.1623776 (PMID 40904356)
takeaway: Empirical evidence that decentralized/community-embedded site models materially increase randomization rate versus traditional academic-site models — direct DCT-outcomes data point.

citation: Integrating mHealth Innovations into Decentralized Oncology Trials. J Med Syst. 2025. DOI: 10.1007/s10916-025-02233-9 (PMID 40779078)
takeaway: Applied review of mobile-health tooling integration into DCT oncology conduct — informs OSSICRO's remote-monitoring/eCOA integration scope.

citation: Assessing the Financial Value of Decentralized Clinical Trials. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2022. DOI: 10.1007/s43441-022-00454-5 (PMID 36104654)
takeaway: Quantitative economic modeling of DCT cost/value versus traditional site-based conduct — directly relevant to the cost/cycle-time bucket of the sweep.

citation: The Impact of Protocol Amendments on Clinical Trial Performance and Cost. Ther Innov Regul Sci (formerly Drug Inf J). 2016. DOI: 10.1177/2168479016632271 (PMID 30227022)
takeaway: Tufts CSDD-lineage analysis quantifying how each protocol amendment adds measurable cost and cycle-time — core citation for the cost/cycle-time bucket.

citation: Quantifying Site Burden to Optimize Protocol Performance. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2024. DOI: 10.1007/s43441-023-00602-5 (PMID 38191957)
takeaway: Direct site-activation/site-burden metrics study; the closest hit to 'site activation cost and cycle time' in the entire sweep — flags protocol-design levers that reduce site burden.

citation: Understanding the Added Value of Action Limits for QTL Monitoring. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2026. DOI: 10.1007/s43441-026-01007-w (PMID 42390815)
takeaway: Current risk-based quality management (RBQM) methodology paper on Quality Tolerance Limits — directly informs an OSSICRO risk-based monitoring/QTL module design.

citation: Quantifying Eligibility Pattern Shifts: a Data-Driven Paradigm for Early Risk Detection in Clinical Trials. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2026. DOI: 10.1007/s43441-026-00918-y (PMID 41649747)
takeaway: Early-warning analytics on drifting eligibility patterns as a trial-conduct risk signal — a monitoring-dashboard feature idea for OSSICRO.

citation: Real-Time, Risk-Based Clinical Trial Quality Management in China: Development of a Digital Monitoring Platform. JMIR Med Inform. 2025. DOI: 10.2196/64114 (PMID 40279500)
takeaway: Description of a built RBQM software platform — closest existing analog/comparator system to an OSSICRO monitoring module, worth studying as prior art.

citation: Adoption Maturity Model for Risk-based Quality Management (RBQM) in Clinical Trials. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2025. DOI: 10.1007/s43441-025-00746-6 (PMID 39953206)
takeaway: Maturity-model framework for RBQM adoption across sponsors/sites — useful as an OSSICRO capability-maturity self-assessment rubric.

citation: Descriptive Analysis of Good Clinical Practice Inspection Findings from U.S. FDA and European Medicines Agency. Ther Innov Regul Sci. 2022. DOI: 10.1007/s43441-022-00417-w (PMID 35610469)
takeaway: Comparative FDA-vs-EMA GCP inspection-finding taxonomy — a direct source for building an OSSICRO 'common deficiency' checklist mapped to actual regulator citation categories.

citation: Recent changes in quality in Japanese clinical trials. Ann Pharmacother. 2004. DOI: 10.1345/aph.1D155 (PMID 14742810)
takeaway: Historical account of a national GCP-quality reform (Japan's 'shin-GCP') driven by inspection findings — comparative regulatory-history reference.

citation: Sariola S, et al. Big-pharmaceuticalisation: clinical trials and Contract Research Organisations in India. Soc Sci Med. 2015;131:239-246. DOI: 10.1016/j.socscimed.2014.11.052 (PMID 25476783)
takeaway: Ethnographic/interview-based study of CRO growth and outsourcing economics in India post-TRIPS — CRO market/economics evidence with a research-ethics angle relevant to micro-CRO governance.

citation: Smed M, Getz KA. Unfulfilled translation opportunities in industry sponsored clinical trials. Contemp Clin Trials. 2013;35(1):80-86. DOI: 10.1016/j.cct.2013.02.007 (PMID 23454567)
takeaway: 451-site-representative survey (Getz/Tufts CSDD-linked) on how CRO intermediation degrades site-to-sponsor knowledge transfer — CRO-model economics and quality-cost tradeoff evidence.

citation: Schnitzbauer AA, et al. Challenges in the organization of investigator initiated trials: in transplantation medicine [German]. Chirurg. 2011;82(3):249-254. DOI: 10.1007/s00104-010-1997-0 (PMID 21416397)
takeaway: Concrete IIT-organization case study (SiLVER trial, 45 sites/13 nations) covering financing, infrastructure, and CRO-delegation choices facing an academic sponsor — a real-world sponsor-investigator operating model.

citation: Drabu S, Gupta A, Bhadauria A. Emerging trends in contract research industry in India. Contemp Clin Trials. 2010;31(5):419-422. DOI: 10.1016/j.cct.2010.06.008 (PMID 20609394)
takeaway: Early (2010) market-sizing snapshot of the CRO industry (~$18bn global 2008, 14% CAGR) with India-specific growth trajectory — historical CRO market-economics data point.

citation: Hecker SJ, Preston C, Foote M. Production of high-quality marketing applications: strategies for biotechnology companies working with contract research organizations. Biotechnol Annu Rev. 2003;9:269-277. DOI: 10.1016/s1387-2656(03)09006-9 (PMID 14650931)
takeaway: Early practitioner account of best-practice CRO/sponsor collaboration for regulatory-submission document production — historical CRO-quality-economics reference.

citation: Rettig RA. The industrialization of clinical research. Health Aff (Millwood). 2000;19(2):129-146. DOI: 10.1377/hlthaff.19.2.129 (PMID 10718027)
takeaway: Foundational health-policy account of clinical research's shift into a 'line of business' and the concomitant rise of the CRO industry — the historical-origin paper for the CRO-market bucket.

citation: Recruitment and Retention of Patients With Ischemic Heart Disease Reporting Hopelessness in a Randomized Controlled Trial. Nurs Res. 2026. DOI: 10.1097/NNR.0000000000000896 (PMID 41705728)
takeaway: Recent methods paper on retention strategies for a psychologically vulnerable cardiac cohort — practical recruitment/retention tactics.

citation: The Psychology Behind the Efficacy of a Buddy System for Enhancing Retention in Dermatological Clinical Trials. Cureus. 2024;16:e72986. DOI: 10.7759/cureus.72986 (PMID 39640154)
takeaway: Social-support ('buddy system') intervention shown to improve trial retention — a low-cost retention-design pattern OSSICRO could template for patient engagement.

citation: Dropout Rate of Participants in Randomized Clinical Trials That Use Virtual Reality to Train Balance and Gait in Parkinson's Disease: A Systematic Review With Meta-analysis and Meta-regression. J Med Syst. 2023. DOI: 10.1007/s10916-023-01930-7 (PMID 37010723)
takeaway: Meta-analytic dropout-rate benchmarking for a technology-delivered intervention arm — quantitative retention baseline useful for power/attrition planning.

citation: Recruiting an underserved, difficult to reach population into a cancer trial: Strategies from the RESTORE Rehabilitation Trial for gay and bisexual prostate cancer patients. Clin Trials. 2022. DOI: 10.1177/17407745221077678 (PMID 35232299)
takeaway: Concrete recruitment-strategy case study for a historically excluded population — supports equity-oriented recruitment-planning guidance in OSSICRO.

citation: Digital and virtual interventions for migraine: A systematic review of randomized controlled trials. Cephalalgia. 2026. DOI: 10.1177/03331024261422276 (PMID 41776980)
takeaway: Systematic review of virtual/digital trial delivery models in one disease area — a comparator for virtual-trial design patterns and outcome consistency.

citation: Hybrid Hospital at Home and Physical Activity for Adults Admitted to the Hospital With Acute Illness: A Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Netw Open. 2026. DOI: 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2026.18812 (PMID 42329654)
takeaway: Recent RCT of a hybrid decentralized/in-home care-delivery model with trial-outcome data — evidences hybrid DCT feasibility at scale.
